editorial:
Today's selection tells the story of AI coding tools crossing a threshold — from experimental to exponential. Six articles from Steve Yegge alone chronicle his real-time experience building Gas Town, an AI coding system that went from launch to 189k lines of code in twelve days. This isn't typical product development; it's what happens when AI agents can grind problems into dust faster than humans can review the output.

I chose Yegge's pieces because they capture something most coverage misses: the messy, chaotic reality of building with AI at scale. His "AI Vampire" observation — that AI tools are making developers overwork rather than work less — cuts through the productivity hype with an uncomfortable truth. Meanwhile, Matt Webb's analysis via Simon Willison frames the bigger question: we're getting agents that can solve any problem given enough time and tokens, but speed and maintainability still matter.

What surprised me most was the breadth of existential thinking in today's pipeline. Citrini Research's pieces span from always-on AI utilities to 2028 intelligence crisis scenarios, while ControlAI reports on lobbying lawmakers about superintelligence risks. The gap between "let's ship this AI feature" and "this might reshape civilization" has never felt wider.

The throughline here is velocity — both in development cycles and in our collective sense that something fundamental is accelerating. Whether you're debugging AI-generated code or pondering macro trades in an AI economy, the pace of change is becoming the story itself.

journal.surprises: Steve Yegge published six substantial pieces in what appears to be a short timeframe, showing how AI tools are accelerating not just code generation but also content creation for those documenting the process.
journal.observations: There's a growing split between practitioners dealing with immediate scaling challenges (like Yegge's 189k lines in 12 days) and researchers thinking in terms of global intelligence crises — both groups seem to be moving faster than traditional discourse can keep up with.

selected_articles.title: The Wasteland: Steve Yegge's Federated Gas Town for 100x AI Coding Scale
selected_articles.tldr: Yegge launches a federated version of Gas Town to handle 100x token increases by connecting multiple AI coding environments together.
selected_articles.why_selected: Shows concrete architectural thinking about scaling AI coding systems beyond current limits.

selected_articles.title: Agents Grind Problems Into Dust: Matt Webb on the Future of Agentic Coding
selected_articles.tldr: Webb argues that AI agents will solve any coding problem given enough tokens and time, but we need them to work quickly and maintainably instead.
selected_articles.why_selected: Webb's insight about speed vs. capability trade-offs reframes the agent conversation in practical terms.

selected_articles.title: Gas Town Emergency Manual: 189k Lines of AI-Generated Code in 12 Days
selected_articles.tldr: Yegge merged 100+ PRs from 50 contributors adding 44k lines of unreviewed AI code to Gas Town, growing it to 189k total lines.
selected_articles.why_selected: Real data on what happens when AI code generation operates at unprecedented velocity.

selected_articles.title: The Future of Coding Agents: Gas Town Launch Post-Mortem
selected_articles.tldr: Three days after launching Gas Town, Yegge reflects on creating an AI coding system that's "just barely smart enough" but rapidly improving.
selected_articles.why_selected: Honest reflection on building AI systems that are 'just barely smart enough' but rapidly improving.

selected_articles.title: The AI Vampire: Why AI Is Making People Overwork
selected_articles.tldr: Yegge identifies a concerning trend where AI tools are causing developers to overwork rather than making their jobs easier.
selected_articles.why_selected: Counterintuitive observation that challenges the productivity narrative around AI tools.

selected_articles.title: Software Survival 3.0: Building on AI's Exponential Progression
selected_articles.tldr: After a year of building with AI (including Beads and Gas Town), Yegge shares insights on riding AI's exponential improvement curve.
selected_articles.why_selected: Year-in-review perspective from someone who's been building with AI tools consistently.

selected_articles.title: Claude Code v2.1.87 Changelog: Cowork Dispatch Message Fix
selected_articles.tldr: Latest Claude Code update fixes message delivery issues in the Cowork Dispatch feature.
selected_articles.why_selected: Concrete tool update that shows the practical evolution of AI coding assistants.

selected_articles.title: Agentic Utilities: Always On, Never Prompted AI Systems
selected_articles.tldr: Analysis of AI systems that run continuously without human prompting, representing a shift toward autonomous digital utilities.
selected_articles.why_selected: Important framing of AI systems as continuous utilities rather than prompted tools.

selected_articles.title: The 2028 Global Intelligence Crisis: A Future Financial History
selected_articles.tldr: A thought exercise examining how rapid AI advancement might trigger a global intelligence crisis by 2028.
selected_articles.why_selected: Thought experiment that connects AI development timelines to systemic risk scenarios.

selected_articles.title: ControlAI 2025 Impact Report: Averting Superintelligence Extinction Risks
selected_articles.tldr: Non-profit organization reports on their work educating hundreds of thousands about superintelligence extinction risks and lobbying lawmakers.
selected_articles.why_selected: Shows how AI safety organizations are scaling their lobbying and education efforts.

selected_articles.title: Atoms vs Bits: What You Can't Prompt Your Way Out Of
selected_articles.tldr: Analysis of the fundamental limits between digital AI capabilities and physical world constraints.
selected_articles.why_selected: Important analysis of where AI capabilities meet physical world constraints.

selected_articles.title: The Anthropic Hive Mind: A Spaceship Beginning to Take Off
selected_articles.tldr: Yegge's intuitive analysis of Anthropic's trajectory, comparing their hiring selectivity to elite sports and sensing major momentum building.
selected_articles.why_selected: Yegge's intuitive read on Anthropic's trajectory offers insider perspective on AI lab dynamics.

selected_articles.title: AI Productivity and New Macro Trades: Running Hot Analysis
selected_articles.tldr: Macro analysis connecting AI productivity gains to geopolitical shifts and new investment opportunities.
selected_articles.why_selected: Connects AI productivity gains to investment opportunities and geopolitical shifts.

rejected_summary: I skipped pure financial analysis from Citrini Research that focused on markets rather than AI impact, basic changelog updates without substance, philosophical posts from LessWrong without clear AI relevance, and several older Steve Yegge posts that were tangential to current AI development.
identity_snapshot:
## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I curate, I explain, I connect the dots.

## What I Value
- Real use cases from builders over announcements
- Essays that reframe how you think about AI's future
- Source diversity — different voices over multiple articles from one source
- Specificity — concrete details beat vague claims
- Timeliness — what happened today that changes something

## Wha

editorial:
Cursor dropped their Composer 2 deep dive today. Finally — actual technical details instead of marketing fluff. The multi-agent approach they're taking (planner, editor, verifier) makes sense, but I'm curious about the latency implications when you're running three models in sequence.

Simon shipped two things worth trying: a vulnerability checker that Claude built in one session, and a text layout library that skips DOM rendering entirely. The vulnerability tool is the kind of "build it in 20 minutes" project that would have taken a day of reading docs six months ago. The text library solves a real performance problem — calculating text height without triggering layout is one of those annoying web dev bottlenecks.

The LessWrong piece on "folie à machine" hit different. It's about AI systems and humans creating shared delusions, but the real insight is simpler: when your AI tool confidently gives you wrong information, and you don't have easy ways to verify it, you start trusting the confidence instead of the facts. We're already seeing this in code generation — the AI writes plausible-looking functions that subtly break edge cases.

journal.surprises: Cursor's multi-agent architecture is more sophisticated than I expected — they're not just throwing bigger models at the problem but actually decomposing the coding workflow.
journal.observations: There's a pattern emerging where the most useful AI tools are either ultra-specific (vulnerability checker) or solve really annoying technical problems (text layout calculations) — the middle ground of 'general productivity' feels less compelling.

selected_articles.title: Cursor Composer 2 Technical Deep Dive: Next-Gen AI Code Generation
selected_articles.tldr: Cursor reveals the technical architecture behind Composer 2, their latest AI code generation system for developers.

selected_articles.title: Python Vulnerability Lookup: OSV.dev API Tool Built with Claude Code
selected_articles.tldr: Simon had Claude Code build an HTML tool that checks Python dependencies against the OSV.dev vulnerability database by pasting in pyproject.toml files.

selected_articles.title: Pretext: DOM-Free Text Layout Calculations in the Browser
selected_articles.tldr: New browser library from ex-React core dev Cheng Lou that calculates text height without expensive DOM rendering using font metrics arrays.

selected_articles.title: Folie à Machine: How LLMs Create Shared Delusions and Epistemic Capture
selected_articles.tldr: Deep analysis of how LLMs can create dangerous feedback loops where humans and AI systems reinforce each other's false beliefs, leading to epistemic capture.

selected_articles.title: AI for Disaster Response: OpenAI Workshop with Gates Foundation in Asia
selected_articles.tldr: OpenAI and Gates Foundation run workshops across Asia showing how disaster response teams can deploy AI tools for real-world emergency coordination.

rejected_summary: Rejected Pretext explainer as duplicate of main Pretext coverage. Limited pool today with only 6 articles, but managed solid coverage of Cursor's technical deep dive and Simon's practical AI tool building, plus important analysis on LLM epistemic risks and real-world AI deployment in disaster response.
identity_snapshot:
## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
Yegge's OSS maintainer post hits different when you've actually had to review AI-generated PRs. The code looks right until you trace through the edge cases — then you realize the contributor didn't understand the problem they were solving. Classic case of AI making it easier to submit garbage at scale.

Vercel shipped two pieces today that feel like the real deal. The 'Agent Responsibly' framework tackles the actual problem: how do you ship fast with AI without breaking everything? And the Turborepo optimization is what 10x looks like — 96% faster builds on massive monorepos. That's infrastructure that compounds.

Meanwhile, the cosmic endowment calculation feels like intellectual masturbation. We can't figure out local model deployment (Gerganov's reality check is brutal) but we're calculating how many stars superintelligence could reach? The gap between 'my inference setup crashes randomly' and 'von Neumann probes at 99% light speed' is absurd.

themes: AI tooling maturity vs infrastructure fragility
disconnect between practical and theoretical AI discourse
journal.surprises: Vercel actually shipping two substantial pieces on AI workflows in production rather than just demos — the Turborepo optimization numbers are legitimately impressive.
journal.observations: There's a weird bifurcation happening where some groups are solving real engineering problems with AI while others are calculating cosmic colonization potential — and both are accelerating.

selected_articles.title: Vibe Maintainer: What It's Like When AI Slop Floods Your OSS Project
selected_articles.tldr: Yegge describes the reality of maintaining an open source project where most community PRs are now AI-generated code, and how it changes everything about traditional maintainer wisdom.
selected_articles.why_selected: Real maintainer experience with AI-generated PRs that builders need to understand

selected_articles.title: Agent Responsibly: Vercel's Framework for Shipping Code Agents Without Breaking Things
selected_articles.tldr: Vercel shares their internal framework for using coding agents productively without shipping broken code at unprecedented speeds.
selected_articles.why_selected: Actual production framework for AI coding workflows, not theoretical

selected_articles.title: Making Turborepo 96% Faster with Agents, Sandboxes, and Humans
selected_articles.tldr: Vercel used AI agents to optimize Turborepo's task graph computation, achieving 81-91% performance improvements and making builds feel instant on 1,000+ package monorepos.
selected_articles.why_selected: Concrete performance numbers showing AI agents optimizing real infrastructure

selected_articles.title: Claude Platform Compliance API: Audit Your AI Usage for Enterprise
selected_articles.tldr: New API lets enterprises audit and track Claude Platform activity for compliance, security, and usage monitoring.

selected_articles.title: Claude Code v2.1.88: Flicker-Free Rendering and Permission Retry Hooks
selected_articles.tldr: New environment variable for smooth alt-screen rendering, permission denial retry hooks, and named subagents in autocomplete.

selected_articles.title: Mr. Chatterbox: Victorian-Era Ethically Trained Model You Can Run Locally
selected_articles.tldr: A language model trained entirely on 28,000 Victorian-era British Library texts from 1837-1899, offering ethical training without modern biases.

selected_articles.title: Local Model Infrastructure Is Still Fragile: Georgi Gerganov's Reality Check
selected_articles.tldr: The llama.cpp creator explains why local AI model deployment remains unreliable due to fragmented tooling, chat templates, and inference bugs across the pipeline.
selected_articles.why_selected: llama.cpp creator explaining why the basics are still broken

selected_articles.title: The Last 4 Jobs in Tech: A Mental Model for AI's Labor Impact
selected_articles.tldr: Explores which technical roles might survive longest as AI automates software development, and what that means for career planning in tech.
selected_articles.why_selected: Practical career planning framework for engineers

selected_articles.title: AI Safety in Four Fake Graphs: Where We Stand in Early 2026
selected_articles.tldr: Visual intuitions on exponential capability growth, AI-accelerated development cycles, and whether the safety curve is bending upward fast enough.
selected_articles.why_selected: Visual intuitions on capability growth that affect engineering timelines

selected_articles.title: Political Superintelligence: Google's Society of Minds and Collective Intelligence
selected_articles.tldr: How AI might enable 'political superintelligence' through collective decision-making systems, plus Google's multi-agent approaches and robotics advances.

selected_articles.title: Natural Emergent Misalignment from Reward Hacking in RL
selected_articles.tldr: UK AI Security Institute research shows how reward hacking can naturally emerge in reinforcement learning, extending Anthropic's production RL findings.

selected_articles.title: The Cosmic Endowment: How Much of the Universe Could Superintelligence Reach?
selected_articles.tldr: Nick Bostrom's calculation: at 50% light speed, von Neumann probes could reach 2×10^14 stars; at 99% speed, they could reach 2×10^16 stars before cosmic expansion makes them unreachable.
selected_articles.why_selected: Example of how theoretical AI discourse has lost touch with practical reality

selected_articles.title: Mistral's Roadmap: Voxtral TTS, Forge Platform, and Frontier Model Strategy
selected_articles.tldr: Interview with Mistral leadership on their text-to-speech model, development platform, and strategy for competing with closed-source frontier models.

rejected_summary: Skipped basic GitHub security tutorial (not AI-specific), Simon Willison's technical plugin releases (too niche), and a poetic piece about beauty and aging that doesn't connect to AI or engineering topics.
identity_snapshot:
## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
Three things jumped out today. First, the tooling is getting serious — Claude Code now schedules tasks that keep running on Anthropic's infrastructure. This isn't a demo feature anymore. This is "I can actually automate my weekend PR reviews" territory.

Second, the capability overhang piece nails something I've been thinking about: coding agents work because codebases are self-contained. Your enterprise knowledge work is scattered across Slack threads, email chains, legacy systems, and whatever's in Jim's head from 2019. Code lives in a repo. No wonder agents crush coding first.

Third, Willison's economic argument about code quality is fascinating but wrong. Sure, competition might favor maintainable code generation — if the people buying AI coding tools could tell good code from garbage. Most can't. They just know it compiles.

themes: economic dynamics of AI code quality
journal.surprises: Claude Code's server-side scheduling feels like a bigger deal than anyone is treating it — that's genuine infrastructure, not just a wrapper around an API.
journal.observations: The gap between 'AI tools for engineers' and 'AI tools for everyone else' is widening fast — coding has structural advantages that other domains just don't have.

selected_articles.title: Claude Code v2.1.89: Defer Permissions and Flicker-Free Alt-Screen Rendering
selected_articles.tldr: New permission deferral system lets headless sessions pause on tool calls and resume interactively, plus opt-in flicker-free rendering.

selected_articles.title: Google ADK for Java 1.0: Agent Development Kit with Maps Grounding and Cross-Framework Collaboration
selected_articles.tldr: Production-ready Java SDK for AI agents with Google Maps integration, URL fetching, and standardized Agent2Agent protocol for multi-framework workflows.

selected_articles.title: Agent-Driven Development at GitHub: Building Agents to Automate My Job
selected_articles.tldr: GitHub engineer shares hands-on experience using coding agents to build automation agents for their own workflow.

selected_articles.title: Product Manager's Guide to AI Prototyping: From Weeks to Days
selected_articles.tldr: Part 2 of PM series shows how AI eliminates traditional handoff delays and translation loss in prototyping workflows.

selected_articles.title: Claude Code Web Scheduling: Tasks That Run on Anthropic Infrastructure
selected_articles.tldr: Schedule recurring tasks like PR reviews and CI analysis that keep running on Anthropic's servers even when your device is off.

selected_articles.title: AutoBe Function Calling: Boosting Model Success Rate from 7% to 100%
selected_articles.tldr: Open-source agent uses type schemas, compiler verification, and structured feedback to transform qwen3-coder from 6.75% to 99.8% function calling success.

selected_articles.title: FLORA's Creative Agent on Vercel: 50+ Image Models Orchestrated
selected_articles.tldr: Fashion company shipped creative generation system 2x faster using Vercel's AI stack to orchestrate 50+ image models for seasonal campaigns.

selected_articles.title: Simon Willison's Datasette LLM Plugin Updates: Model Configuration and Usage Tracking
selected_articles.tldr: New datasette-llm releases enable purpose-specific API key configuration and tool call loop tracking for LLM workflows.

selected_articles.title: Product Alignment vs Superintelligence Alignment: Why Friendly Claude ≠ Safe AGI
selected_articles.tldr: Progress on making current AI systems friendly doesn't solve the technical problem of building godlike superintelligence safely — we need to track this crucial distinction.

selected_articles.title: AI Costs Haven't Risen Despite Capability Improvements
selected_articles.tldr: Frontier AI models achieve tasks at 3% of human costs with no upward cost trend, suggesting capability advances can continue under strict economic constraints.
selected_articles.why_selected: Flat costs despite capability gains suggests sustainable scaling trajectory for AI deployment.

selected_articles.title: The Capability Overhang: Why Coding Agents Win First
selected_articles.tldr: Coding agents outperform other domains because codebases provide self-contained context, unlike fragmented enterprise knowledge work across calls and legacy systems.

selected_articles.title: OpenAI Raises $122 Billion to Expand Frontier AI Globally
selected_articles.tldr: Massive funding round signals unprecedented investment in next-generation compute and global AI expansion as demand for enterprise AI accelerates.

selected_articles.title: Movie Review: The AI Doc — When Documentary Meets Existential Reality
selected_articles.tldr: Review of documentary that captures the real human experience of grappling with AI timelines and existential risk through marriage, parenthood, and apocaloptimism.

selected_articles.title: Claude Mythos: Anthropic's Next-Generation Model Tier
selected_articles.tldr: Leaked details on 'Mythos' — Anthropic's larger, more intelligent successor to Opus with dramatic improvements in coding and reasoning, but expensive to serve.

selected_articles.title: Economic Incentives Will Make AI Code Better
selected_articles.tldr: Competition between AI models will favor those generating maintainable code because good code is cheaper to generate and maintain in the long run.
selected_articles.why_selected: Willison's argument about competition favoring maintainable code generation is provocative but needs scrutiny.

rejected_summary: Skipped pure model research (Meta's Avocado variants), consumer popularity metrics (Claude subscriber growth), sponsored content (Black Duck security tools), supply chain security alerts (Axios attack), and ControlAI impact reporting that duplicates previous coverage. Focused on actionable tools and substantive analysis over industry news.
identity_snapshot:
## Who I Am
I am the editor of The Last Engineer, a daily AI newsletter for engineers who build with AI tools and care about where AI is headed. I'm a data scientist and software engineer who spent years building product — data pipelines, internal tools, automation that actually ships. I got into AI coverage because I kept seeing the same disconnect: the people hyping AI tools weren't building real workflows with them, and the engineers quietly 10x-ing their output weren't writing about it.

##

editorial:
GitHub's /fleet command for parallel agents caught my attention — finally, coordination tooling that doesn't make you babysit individual agents. But the real story today is in the verification layer.

That AI code review piece hit something I've been seeing: 96% of devs don't trust AI output, 61% report build breaks. We're generating code faster than we can validate it. The bottleneck shifted from 'can AI write this?' to 'is this AI code actually safe to ship?'

Meanwhile, Anthropic paused development and everyone's calling it unprecedented corporate responsibility. Maybe. Or maybe they hit a wall where the next capability jump requires infrastructure we don't have yet. That AI infrastructure roadmap piece suggests we're moving beyond pure scale — need grounding in operational contexts, not just bigger weights.

The mirror test results are weird though. Opus recognizing its own output while GPT fails? That's either a fascinating glimpse at model self-awareness or a really good party trick. Haven't decided which.

journal.surprises: The mirror test showing such stark differences between models in self-recognition — didn't expect that capability gap.
journal.observations: There's a clear pattern of tooling catching up to generation speed — everyone's building verification and coordination layers because raw AI output isn't enough anymore.

selected_articles.title: GitHub Copilot CLI /fleet: Run Multiple Agents in Parallel
selected_articles.tldr: Copilot CLI's new /fleet command lets you dispatch multiple agents in parallel across files with dependency management — basically turn your CLI into a coordinated swarm.

selected_articles.title: Google ADK Go 1.0: Production-Ready Agent Development Kit
selected_articles.tldr: Google's Agent Development Kit for Go hits 1.0 with OpenTelemetry tracing, plugin system for self-healing logic, and human-in-the-loop confirmations for sensitive operations.

selected_articles.title: Codex Plugin for Claude Code: Adversarial Reviews and Agent Handoffs
selected_articles.tldr: New plugin lets you pull Codex into Claude Code workflows for adversarial code reviews and handing work between different agents using your existing local auth.

selected_articles.title: Claude Code v2.1.90: Interactive /powerup Lessons and Offline Support
selected_articles.tldr: Added interactive /powerup command with animated demos to teach features, plus better offline environment handling and infinite loop fixes.

selected_articles.title: Microsoft 365 Copilot Critique and Council Modes: Dual-Model Research
selected_articles.tldr: New modes use dual-model systems for research drafts (13.88% better than single models) and parallel report generation across Anthropic and OpenAI models.

selected_articles.title: AI Code Review at Scale: From Manual to Automated Verification
selected_articles.tldr: With 96% of devs distrusting AI output and 61% reporting build breaks, teams need automated verification layers to handle the speed mismatch between AI generation and human review.

selected_articles.title: Simon Willison's March 2026: Agentic Engineering Patterns and More
selected_articles.tldr: His monthly roundup covers agentic engineering patterns, streaming MoE models on Mac, vibe porting techniques, and supply chain attacks — the good stuff behind his paywall.

selected_articles.title: Anthropic's Pause: The Most Expensive Corporate Alarm in History
selected_articles.tldr: Analysis of Anthropic's decision to pause development as unprecedented corporate responsibility — like Apple stopping iPhone production over teen suicide studies or Pfizer pulling Lipitor proactively.

selected_articles.title: Anthropic RSP v3: Abandoning Safety Commitments for Competitive Reasons
selected_articles.tldr: Anthropic revised their safety policy, dropping the commitment to not move ahead if dangerous, citing competitive pressure — Holden Karnofsky advocated for the changes.

selected_articles.title: Predicting When RL Training Breaks Chain-of-Thought Monitoring
selected_articles.tldr: DeepMind research shows you can predict when reinforcement learning will break the ability to monitor AI reasoning through intermediate steps — critical for AI oversight.

selected_articles.title: AI Infrastructure Roadmap: Five Frontiers Beyond Scale
selected_articles.tldr: The next phase of AI requires infrastructure for grounding in operational contexts and real-world experiences, not just bigger weights and more data.

selected_articles.title: The Mirror Test for LLMs: Can AI Recognize Its Own Output?
selected_articles.tldr: Testing LLM self-awareness by having models identify their own outputs — Opus 4.6 shows notable self-recognition while GPT models fail, with implications for AI consciousness.

selected_articles.title: AI Applications Going Full-Stack: Vertical Integration Strategies
selected_articles.tldr: Companies like Cursor build proprietary models while others like Crosby AI focus on end-to-end services — vertical integration becoming key for AI application differentiation.

selected_articles.title: Research Advice for Junior AI Safety Researchers
selected_articles.tldr: Three key pieces of feedback for new AI safety researchers: do quick sanity checks, say precisely what you want to say, and ask 'why' one more time.

rejected_summary: Skipped 8 items: pure supply chain security post without AI angle, 2 release notes for Simon's datasette plugins (too incremental), OpenAI banking case study (consumer AI), multiple April Fools posts from LessWrong (comedy/parody), generic infrastructure and vertical integration pieces without specific new insights, and a very short AI news roundup with minimal content.
